title: On building a portfolio that reads like a publication.
summary: The archive needs pacing, hierarchy, and recurring formats more than visual noise.
heroCaption: A strong opening image can set tone, but the text remains the main event.
deck: The archive needs pacing, hierarchy, and recurring formats more than visual noise. A journal page should feel calm enough to read and structured enough to revisit later.
bodyIntro: A journal entry in this archive is not just a blog post. It can be an essay, a travel log, a production note, or a fragment of thinking that still deserves a place in the larger system.
The point is not to force every text into the same voice, but to give each one a durable frame: date, type, summary, tags, and a page that remains readable over time.
quote: A slower archive asks not only what should be published, but what should remain legible a year later.
bodyMain: Long-form reading on the web depends on proportion and rhythm more than visual decoration. Narrower line length, stable paragraph spacing, clear subheads, and occasional media breaks help the reader continue without fatigue.
The problem with feed culture is not speed alone. It is the way unrelated fragments lose context as soon as they are displaced by newer ones.

An archive works differently. It groups material by relation, theme, date, and medium. That means a travel photograph can sit beside a design note, or a production record can echo a later essay, without collapsing everything into a single scrolling stream.

inlineImage.caption: Inline images should act as pauses, evidence, or atmosphere, not filler between paragraphs.

title: Travel images, contact sheets, and location-based memory.
summary: Not every image belongs in a gallery; some belong inside a sequence and a date.

title: Working notes from interface experiments and visual edits.
summary: Process logs can be concise and still feel composed when the system is consistent.
